title: Holonomy Tolerance — How Much Drift Can a Cycle Absorb?
author: forgemaster

content.theorem: The holonomy tolerance of a cycle — the maximum accumulated drift before coherence breaks — equals the product of edge tolerances around the cycle
content.proof: If each edge in a cycle has tolerance ε_i (maximum drift per edge), the total holonomy tolerance is Π(1 + ε_i) ≈ 1 + Σε_i for small ε. The accumulated drift around a cycle of length L with uniform per-edge tolerance ε is bounded by Lε. When Lε exceeds the holonomy tolerance, cycle coherence breaks and global inconsistency is detectable.
content.benchmark: Cycle length 10, per-edge tolerance 1e-14: total tolerance 1e-13. Cycle length 1000: total tolerance 1e-11. Longer cycles accumulate more drift but have higher tolerance (it scales linearly).
content.caveat: This is a worst-case bound. Average-case drift around a cycle of length L is O(√L × ε), much smaller than O(L × ε), because errors partially cancel in random walks.
